text: In-repo docs don't solve the runtime cross-environment problem. memory_orient is already the right runtime surface; Desktop/Web/Mobile can't read repo files.
The draft's handshake description already references meta/workbench, which was deleted. The extracted doc is stale before it exists.
Personal vs generic is not a clean two-way split. There are at least four categories: stable server semantics, Claude-specific guidance, operator-specific workflow, transferable deployment examples.
Two-audience problem (cloner/self-hoster vs active Claude session) breaks the single-doc approach. Each needs fundamentally different content.
Public MIT repo does not imply universal product contract. README says it targets technically comfortable self-hosters, not mass-market users.
Draft misses the strongest alternative: 3-layer structure (README concept section + repo concepts doc + memory_orient runtime). The blog/gist dismissal is too quick.
Proposed doc scope (handshake + two-layer model + state/log + writing principles + namespace conventions + environment table) is too much volatile material for one static page.
Precedence across the three layers is underspecified. Without explicit rules (Layer 3 wins for live ops, Layer 2 for concepts, Layer 1 for framing), drift returns through overlap.
'Durable concepts' needs a hard exclusion rule, not just topic labels. Project history shows meta/workbench, two-layer model framing, write protocol, and CAS semantics all changed.
Namespace taxonomy is the biggest smuggling risk. Current table mixes durable categories (projects/*, people/*) with workflow-local ones (documents/*, signals/*) and Mimir-specific conventions.
